title: MM to Carat Conversion Calculator
tagline: Convert gemstone dimensions in millimeters into an estimated carat weight for diamonds, colored stones, pearls, and more.
intro: Enter the stone dimensions, choose a gemstone type, and get an instant estimated carat weight, stone volume, and a practical +/-10% range.
formula: Formula: Carat Weight = (Length × Width × Depth × specific gravity) / 1000

error.invalid: Enter a positive length, width, and depth, then choose a gemstone type.

about.heading: About the mm to carat conversion calculator
about.body:
Millimeter-to-carat conversion is one of the quickest ways to estimate gemstone weight when you only have dimensions and no scale reading. In jewelry buying, loose-stone sourcing, custom setting work, and gemology training, people often know the length, width, and depth of a stone long before they know its exact weight. This calculator bridges that gap by translating those dimensions into an estimated carat value using the standard industry relationship between volume and specific gravity.

Carat is a unit of mass equal to 200 milligrams, but gemstones are usually described by face-up size first because that is what buyers see. Two stones that share the same diameter can still weigh differently if their depths differ or if they are made from materials with different densities. That is why the formula multiplies the stone volume by a specific gravity constant. Diamond, sapphire, ruby, emerald, opal, pearl, and other gems do not all pack the same mass into the same physical space, so the material choice matters just as much as the measurements.

This tool uses a simplified rectangular-volume approximation: length times width times depth. That makes it especially practical for quick estimates, inventory planning, sourcing conversations, online listings, or educational comparisons across gem types. In real cutting practice, shape, pavilion depth, girdle thickness, culet size, and faceting style can all push the actual weight above or below the estimate. A round brilliant, oval, princess, or cabochon with the same outside dimensions may not carry exactly the same carat weight because the internal geometry is different.

The displayed +/-10% range is useful because it reflects the fact that dimension-based estimates are directional rather than laboratory-grade measurements. If you are evaluating a stone for pricing, shipping insurance, or mount compatibility, the range gives you a practical envelope for decision-making before the stone is weighed. It can also help you compare whether one listing looks unusually heavy or unusually shallow for its stated size.

Use this calculator as a fast planning tool for jewelry design, gem comparison, and rough valuation. When precision is critical, always confirm the final carat weight with a calibrated scale or trusted gem report. Even so, a good dimensional estimate is incredibly valuable for buyers, bench jewelers, and students who need a reliable answer in seconds.

examples.heading: MM to carat conversion examples
examples.intro: Three sample stones showing how size and gemstone density change the estimated carat weight.

items.note: A square 6 mm diamond with 4 mm depth and a specific gravity of 3.52 gives an estimated half-carat weight.
Rubies and sapphires use a higher specific gravity than emerald, so a compact stone can still carry meaningful weight.
Emerald is less dense than diamond or ruby, so the same volume usually produces a lower carat estimate.

howto.heading: How to use the mm to carat conversion calculator
steps: Measure the gemstone length, width, and depth in millimeters as accurately as possible.
Choose the gemstone type so the calculator can apply the correct specific gravity value.
Click Calculate to see the estimated carat weight, stone volume, and a practical +/-10% range.
Click Reset to clear the form and compare a different stone size or gemstone material.

faq.heading: MM to carat conversion FAQ
faq.q1: Why is the carat result only an estimate?
faq.q1_a: The calculator uses external dimensions and a simplified volume model. Real gemstone shapes, cutting styles, and proportions vary, so exact weight can shift even when the millimeter measurements are the same.
faq.q2: Why do diamond and emerald give different carat weights for similar sizes?
faq.q2_a: Different gemstones have different specific gravities. Denser materials place more mass into the same volume, so they produce a higher carat estimate.
faq.q3: Can I use this for fancy cuts and cabochons?
faq.q3_a: Yes, but treat the result as a fast planning estimate. Unusual outlines, domed tops, or very deep stones can differ more noticeably from the simplified formula.
faq.q4_a: It provides a practical margin around the estimate to reflect common variation caused by cut depth, girdle thickness, and overall stone geometry.
